summary: Fowler critiques the traditional waterfall assumption that requirements must be discovered before construction begins. He argues that agile methods intentionally discover requirements during and after development, but leading web companies go even further by observing actual user behavior to generate feature ideas. This approach—watching what users actually do rather than what they say they want—provides more direct information about real needs. Fowler advocates for teams to build monitoring capabilities into their applications and use behavioral data to drive product decisions.
key_points: The word 'requirement' itself embeds waterfall assumptions about upfront discovery
Agile methods deliberately discover requirements during construction and after delivery
Web companies pioneer 'observed requirements' by analyzing user behavior logs to identify features
There's a significant gap between what people say they want and what they actually need and use
Building monitoring and analysis capabilities requires concentrated effort but offers huge potential
money_quotes: Agile methods violate this underlying assumption by intending to discover the 'requirements' during construction and after delivery
Build a new feature and see if people use it
Everyone knows how big the difference is between what people say they want and what people actually need and use
By watching what people actually do with your application, you can find out what actually happens with the software - which can give you much more direct information than other sources
key_insight: Observing actual user behavior provides more reliable requirements information than asking users what they want, representing an evolution beyond even agile's iterative discovery approach.
